old_claim.note: Early Symbolic Communication is an earlier historical predecessor or foundation, not a one-to-one engineering dependency.

new_claim.note: Tally marks are a later formalized use of durable graphic marks for quantity or memory, following earlier abstract symbolic marks without implying a direct one-to-one invention chain.

source_shape.source_locator: Review of archaeological numerical markings: situates notched bones and other durable marks within the transition from nonsymbolic quantity sense to material number symbols.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The review frames notched bones and durable quantity marks as part of the archaeological transition into material number symbols.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports early symbolic communication as a predecessor context for tally-like marks, while the lower confidence avoids claiming a direct invention chain from Blombos-style abstract marks to Border Cave notches.

invariant_preserved_or_changed: Preserved: early_symbolic_communication remains a historical predecessor, now with a review-backed rationale and lower confidence.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to purely utilitarian non-symbolic grip marks.
A review showed Paleolithic tally-like marks arose independently of symbolic graphic marking traditions.
The early_symbolic_communication node were narrowed so far that durable abstract marks no longer fit its scope.
short_reason: Tally-like marks are a later durable quantity-marking use within the broader symbolic-mark tradition.
